--- SOURCE 1: Urban Heat Island - (AP Human Geography) - Fiveable ---
URL: https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/ap-hug/urban-heat-island

SUMMARY:
An Urban Heat Island (UHI) is a localized area, typically in a city, where temperatures are significantly higher than the surrounding rural areas.

An Urban Heat Island (UHI) is a phenomenon where a city or metropolitan area is warmer than its surrounding rural region due to human development and

# What is an Urban Heat Island (UHI)?

![A day time bird's eye view of a large city with tall buildings, freeways, streets and some green space sprinkled in. Buildings fill the image with some smog cover. ](/sites/default/files/styles/slideshow_image/public/2022-12/pexels-mikhail-nilov-8299703.jpg?h=fd2bf076&itok=4wVxP19c)

The definition of an Urban Heat Island (UHI) is a phenomenon where a city or metropolitan area is warmer than its surrounding rural region due to human development and activities. The replacement of natural vegetation and land cover with asphalt, concrete, and building material seen in cities increases the energy absorbed at the ground level from sunlight. This energy is then re-radiated into the environment as heat, combined with restricted airflow from tall buildings and urban geography and output from human activity and industry, accumulates over the day. As a result, temperatures within urban areas significantly increase over the day compared to their rural counterparts. This difference can be as much as 24°F locally and is usually at its greatest in the evenings and overnight. Columbus is no exception to this phenomenon. According to a 2014 Climate Central study, Columbus had the 8th most intense UHI in the country, with summertime temperatures rising to 4.4°F warmer in the city on average compared to surrounding rural areas. Columbus also has the fastest-growing UHI in the country, with an increase of 0.84°F per decade since 1970. This impacts residents in numerous ways, including the exacerbation of air pollution; increased energy consumption required to cool homes, schools, and businesses; and higher risk of heat-related ailments, especially for high-risk populations including children, pregnant women, elderly adults, exposed workers, and low-income households or marginalized neighborhoods. Households and communities are most at risk, where swaths of residents are socially isolated and unable to afford household cooling.

While increased daytime high temperatures are the weather impact that gets the most attention, persistently warmer nighttime temperatures caused by UHIs should not be understated. Warm nighttime temperatures increase stress on the body, especially for those without suitable household cooling. Without an adequate break from the heat or a way to cool off, prolonged heat stress into the overnight hours can lead to problems sleeping, dehydration, the exacerbation of pre-existing health conditions such as heart or kidney problems, and even the onset of heat exhaustion or stroke.

Although these impacts have already been experienced in other cities, both in the United States (during the 1995 Chicago Heat Wave) and elsewhere around the globe during high heat events, often with significant fatality rates, researchers are now examining more closely how much of an impact UHIs have on hospitalization trends in the region.

![A street view image of green traffic lights, with sidewalk, trees, buildings, bus and cars  on the left, building straight ahead  and green grass median and park, sidewalks and trees and people walking](/sites/default/files/2022-08/landcover_2_downtown.png)

An urban heat island, or UHI, is a metropolitan area that's a lot warmer than the rural areas surrounding it Downtown New York City is an urban heat island, a built-up area with temperatures that are higher than the rural areas surrounding it. An urban heat island, or UHI, is a metropolitan area that's a lot warmer than the rural areas surrounding it. Urban heat islands are created in areas like these: places that have lots of activity and lots of people. Urban areas are also densely constructed, meaning buildings are constructed very close together. Urban heat islands can have worse air and water quality than their rural neighbors.

Urban Heat Island
=================

Urban Heat Island
=================

An urban heat island, or UHI, is a metropolitan area that's a lot warmer than the rural areas surrounding it

### Grades

5 - 12+

### Subjects

Earth Science, Meteorology, Geography, Human Geography, Physical Geography

Photograph

##### New York Cityscape

Downtown New York City is an urban heat island, a built-up area with temperatures that are higher than the rural areas surrounding it. According to the Environmental Protection Agency, the annual air temperature of a city with 1 million people can be 1.8–5.4 degrees Fahrenheit (1–3 degrees Celsius) warmer than its surroundings.

Photograph by Peggy Fahey, MyShot

![Image 1: New York Cityscape](https://images.nationalgeographic.org/image/upload/t_edhub_resource_key_image/v1638890130/EducationHub/photos/new-york-cityscape.jpg)

![Image 2: New York Cityscape](https://images.nationalgeographic.org/image/upload/t_edhub_attached_res_thumb/v1638890130/EducationHub/photos/new-york-cityscape.jpg)Photograph

![Image 3: Urban Heat Island](https://images.nationalgeographic.org/image/upload/t_edhub_attached_res_thumb/v1638890067/EducationHub/photos/urban-heat-island.jpg)Photograph

Powered by

![Image 5: Morgan Stanley](https://images.nationalgeographic.org/image/upload/v1638892868/EducationHub/logos/ms_logo-13p0_k.png)

[](https://classroom.google.com/share?url=https://education.nationalgeographic.org/resource/urban-heat-island/)[](https://teams.microsoft.com/share?href=https://education.nationalgeographic.org/resource/urban-heat-island/)

Article Vocabulary

An urban heat island, or UHI, is a metropolitan area that's a lot warmer than the rural areas surrounding it. Heat is created by energy from all the people, cars, buses, and trains in big cities like New York, Paris, and London. Urban heat islands are created in areas like these: places that have lots of activity and lots of people.

There are many reasons for UHIs. When houses, shops, and industrial buildings are constructed close together, it can create a UHI. Building materials are usually very good at insulating, or holding in heat. This insulation makes the areas around buildings warmer.

"Waste heat" also contributes to a UHI. People and their tools, such as cars and factories, are always burning off energy, whether they’re jogging, driving, or just living their day-to-day lives. The energy people burn off usually escapes in the form of heat. And if there are a lot of people in one area, that's a lot of heat.

Urban areas are densely populated, meaning there are a lot of people in a small space. Urban areas are also densely constructed, meaning buildings are constructed very close together. When there is no more room for an urban area to expand, engineers build upward, creating skyscrapers. All this construction means waste heat—and heat that escapes insulation has nowhere to go. It lingers in and between buildings in the UHI.

Nighttime temperatures in UHIs remain high. This is because buildings, sidewalks, and parking lots block heat coming from the ground from rising into the cold night sky. Because the heat is trapped on lower levels, the temperature is warmer.

Urban heat islands can have worse air and water quality than their rural neighbors. UHIs often have lower air quality because there are more pollutants (waste products from vehicles, industry, and people) being pumped into the air. These pollutants are blocked from scattering and becoming less toxic by the urban landscape: buildings, roads, sidewalks, and parking lots.

Water quality also suffers. When warm water from the UHI ends up flowing into local streams, it stresses the native species that have adapted to life in a cooler aquatic environment.

Scientists are studying how urban heat islands might contribute to global warming, the most recent climate change pattern that includes the gradual warming of the Earth's temperature.

When it's really hot, many of us run straight to the fan or the air conditioning. This is especially true in urban areas that suffer from urban heat island effects. UHIs contribute to energy demands in the summer, straining energy resources. UHIs are often subject to “rolling blackouts,” or power outages. Utility companies start rolling blackouts when they do not have enough energy to meet their customers’ demands. The energy used in electric fans and air conditioning ends up contributing to an even hotter UHI.

Because of these negative effects, scientists say city dwellers, architects, and designers all have to work to reduce people's impact on urban areas. Using green roofs, which are roofs of buildings covered in plants, helps cool things down. Plants absorb carbon dioxide, a leading pollutant. They also reduce the heat of the surrounding areas. Using lighter-colored materials on buildings helps, too. Light colors reflect more sunlight and trap less heat.

Fast Fact

**Turning Up the Heat**

The Heat Island Group says that the urban heat island around Los Angeles, California, costs the city $100 million a year in energy!

--- SOURCE 4: Urban Heat Island Effect - an overview | ScienceDirect Topics ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/urban-heat-island-effect

SUMMARY:
Urban areas, with their large volume of impermeable pavements, and building surfaces that have low reflectance and increased thermal storage compared with agricultural areas, are subject to an urban heat island effect (Feyisa et al.,2014; Zhu et al.,2015). The Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect is a well-described phenomenon and when its impacts are combined with climate change, it can significantly affect the thermal conditions of cities (Phelan et al., 2015). Due to dark surfaces, human-caused warmth, highly absorbing materials, a lack of green spaces, the trapping of infrared radiation by urban architecture, and low air circulation in urban areas leads to an increase in surface temperature in an urban environment known as UHI effect (Baniassadi et al., 2018, Boafo et al., 2017, Movahed et al., 2021, Bakhshoodeh et al., 2022, McConnell et al., 2022a).

[2018, Construction and Building Materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/construction-and-building-materials)Wei Jiang, ... Aimin Sha

### 4.1 Functional requirements for low heat absorption by pavement

Currently, large cities in the world suffer from the urban heat island effect (i.e. the temperatures in downtown areas are significantly higher than in the suburbs) and the problem is becoming increasingly serious [63,64]. Heat island brings adverse effects on the urban environment in various aspects, such as an increase of energy demand for cooling, which leads to more air pollutants and greenhouse gas emissions, lowered groundwater quality, and endangerment of urban biodiversity and human health [65,66].

Urban heat island is a combined effect of human activities and local [meteorological conditions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/meteorological-condition "Learn more about meteorological conditions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") during urbanization. The causes of urban heat island effect include the characteristics of urban ground surface, greenhouse gas emissions, concentration of heat sources, and air pollution. Roads are a major cause of urban heat island effect [67,68]. Pavement surface in the city, especially [asphalt pavement](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/asphalt-pavements "Learn more about asphalt pavement from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), has changed the original thermal properties of the natural ground surface. The temperature of asphalt pavement surface rises rapidly under solar radiation to 65–70 °C, a temperature that is significantly higher than that of natural ground surface [69,70]. Furthermore, the pavement surface absorbs and stores heat during the day and releases it at night, which aggravates the urban heat island effect [69]. Thus, changing the thermal properties of [pavement materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/pavement-material "Learn more about pavement materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a crucial measure of alleviating the urban heat island effect. For example, using pavement materials with a large thermal resistance coefficient, applying light-colored or heat-reflective [coating materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/coating-material "Learn more about coating materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on road surfaces, as well as using pavement materials with good capacity of absorbing and retaining water are common measures [71]. By reducing the capacity of heat storage, the amount of heat released from the road can be reduced, and the comfort of pedestrians and residents nearby can be improved. Besides, this will also help to reduce [permanent deformation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/permanent-deformation "Learn more about permanent deformation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of [asphalt](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/asphalt "Learn more about asphalt from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") pavement caused by high temperatures and thus, prolong pavement service life [72,73].

Chapter

[The urban heat island effect: causes and potential solutions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780857090461500059)

[2012, Metropolitan Sustainability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/book/9780857090461/metropolitan-sustainability)M.E. Hulley

### 5.5 Conclusions

The urban [heat island](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/urban-heat-island-effect "Learn more about heat island from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") effect represents an important environmental problem facing all large urban centers. Local increases in average annual temperature are well documented, as are the costs and environmental impacts associated with increased cooling. A number of [mitigation measures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/mitigation-measure "Learn more about mitigation measures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are available and generally involve an increase in [surface reflectance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/surface-reflectance "Learn more about surface reflectance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or [albedo](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/albedo "Learn more about albedo from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and an increase in urban vegetation. The measures outlined in this chapter are not exhaustive, but are generally representative of available solutions. Any effective solution will certainly include a combination of measures, including cool pavements, cool roofs, increases in urban forests and green roofs.

Developing and implementing comprehensive [mitigation strategies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/mitigation-strategy "Learn more about mitigation strategies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for urban areas will take considerable time. However, providing we start the process, eventually we will achieve the desired goals – a journey of 1000 miles begins with what’s underfoot. Since enormous investments in infrastructure and buildings have been made with little concern for the consequences in terms of urban heat islands, the final solution will require both a shift in current construction techniques and serious consideration of [retrofit](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/retrofit "Learn more about retrofit from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") options where appropriate. Despite the long-term benefits of cool pavements and cool roofs, [initial capital costs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/initial-capital-cost "Learn more about initial capital costs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are often greater than conventional construction approaches, and some incentives or regulation changes will be necessary in order to encourage the implementation of these mitigation strategies.

Fortunately, estimation of some of the potential benefits of global mitigation strategies (in terms of reductions in average annual temperatures, for example) is possible with screening tools such as MIST. Use of tools like MIST, the findings of other documented [case studies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/case-study "Learn more about case studies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Rosenzweig _et al.,_ 2006) and increased public awareness of the importance of urban heat islands should lead to an acceptance and implementation of site-specific mitigation strategies and, ultimately, improved urban environments.

### 2.2 Urban heat island circulation

Urban [heat island](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/urban-heat-island-effect "Learn more about heat island from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") effect, as a significant form of anthropogenic climate change caused by urbanization and industrialization, has a great impact on [urban climate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/urban-climate "Learn more about urban climate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The urban heat island phenomenon has been known for more than 200 years since Howard's [36] seminal observation in [London](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/london "Learn more about London from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in 1818. Santamouris [37] pointed out that the [urban heat island effect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/urban-heat-island-effect "Learn more about urban heat island effect from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is one of the most obvious features of human's influence on the earth system, and almost all major cities in the world have recorded the warming trend [38]. On average, the temperature in urban areas may be 1–3°C higher than that in rural areas, but under appropriate meteorological conditions (calm nights in winter without clouds), the air temperature may be more than 10°C higher than the rural environment [39]. Although the impact of the urban heat island effect on [global warming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/global-warming "Learn more about global warming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is still controversial [40–44], it is generally believed that the urban heat island effect will affect local and regional climate [45–51].

Urban heat island can induce natural [convective flows](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/convective-flow "Learn more about convective flows from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on the urban scale, namely urban heat island circulation. Under calm and stable weather, a dome-shaped region with high temperature will eventually form on the section. Therefore, urban heat island circulations are also commonly referred to as urban dome flow [52]. Barlag and Kuttler [53] and Eliasson and Holmer [30] found that the frequency of urban heat island circulation is about 10% during the year, and it is higher at low latitudes. Although urban heat island circulations can occur during the day, night and throughout the year [30,54,55], they are more intense at midnight in winter when there is a strong surface inversion [56]. According to the field measurement data, Hidalgo et al. [57] found that the intensity of urban heat island circulation would increase from 2 m/s at noon to 5–6 m/s in the evening. Urban heat island circulations are characterized as the convergent inflow (from the rural area to the urban area) at the lower level, the upward flow (dominated by a vertical velocity component) over the urban centre and the divergent outflow (from the urban area to the rural area) at the upper level, which is strongly supported by the results of previous studies [58–61]. Its flow pattern will also change with the change of urban form [62,63]. Urban heat island circulation firstly transports [urban pollutants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/urban-pollutant "Learn more about urban pollutants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), heat and water out through its upward flow and divergence flow, and then brings these rural elements into urban areas through convergence flow [64]. Therefore, the study of urban heat island circulation has not only important scientific value but also important practical significance.

### 18.3.3 Climate Change

Urban areas, with their large volume of impermeable pavements, and building surfaces that have low reflectance and increased thermal storage compared with agricultural areas, are subject to an urban [heat island](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/urban-heat-island-effect "Learn more about heat island from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") effect (Feyisa et al.,2014; Zhu et al.,2015). Furthermore, urban areas have a large energy demand for the cooling and heating systems of various buildings and facilities (Jo et al.,2009). The [urban heat island effect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/urban-heat-island-effect "Learn more about urban heat island effect from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is reflected in the shallow subsurface and hence groundwater temperature (Taniguchi et al.,2007; Zhu et al.,2015). Over the last five decades (1970–2014), the annual mean air temperature of the city showed a marked increasing trend of +0.03 o C/y, which is closely related to the urban heat island effect and global climate change (warming) (Figure 18.6).

[2018, Nature Based Strategies for Urban and Building Sustainability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/book/9780128121504/nature-based-strategies-for-urban-and-building-sustainability)Haibo Feng, Kasun N. Hewage

### Mitigation of Urban Heat Island Effect

In urban environments, vegetation has often been replaced by impervious and dark surfaces. Dark surfaces reflect less solar radiation and absorb more energy. Due to the lack of vegetation and the presence of dark surfaces, the urban heat island effect is created. A simulation study in New York showed that the average roof temperature can be reduced by as much as 0.8°C if 50% of the roof area is covered with vegetation (Rosenzweig et al., 2006). In Venice, the field observation and simulations results showed that the temperature of a green [permeable surface](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/permeable-surface "Learn more about permeable surface from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") could be 4°C lower than the existing paved roof (Peron et al., 2015). It was also estimated that the urban heat island effect can be reduced by 1–2 degrees Celsius if 6% of Toronto was covered with green vegetation (Peak, 2004). Another report on the Mediterranean region shows that 10%–14% of the electrical energy consumed in cooling residential buildings can be saved by green roofs (Zinzi and Agnoli, 2012). Green roof performance in reducing the urban heat island effect varies in different locations, due to the conditions in the surrounding environment, and changes in building density.

### 2.1 Introduction

The urban [heat island](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/urban-heat-island-effect "Learn more about heat island from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (UHI) effect is a common environmental problem occurring in metropolitan areas in which the air temperature is significantly higher than in suburban areas. The UHI effect also leads to a smoggy climate. The UHI effect mainly results from high-density infrastructures, which not only limit air flow but also emit heat stored from solar energy. In addition, the reduction of vegetation and wetlands further weakens the heat-releasing capacity of cities.It is urgent to mitigate the UHI effect, as it has become a huge threat to the environment and human health ([Santamouris](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/santamouris "Learn more about Santamouris from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), 2007; Santamouris et al., 2011; Stathopoulou, 2008; Mirzaei and Haghighat, 2010).

Previous studies indicated that the optical and thermal characteristics of urban structures have a strong relationship with [urban climate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/urban-climate "Learn more about urban climate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and temperatures (Chen et al., 2009; White et al., 2010). In many cities, the roofs and pavements comprise about 60% of the total urban area, with roofs contributing about 20–25% and pavements contributing about 40% (Akbari et al., 2003). Cantat indicated that the reflectance of Paris is 16% lower than the surrounding suburban district (Cantat, 1989). Another study claimed that the increase of the solar energy absorption in urban areas is affected by the urban geometrical structures (Aida, 1982; Aida and Gotoh, 1982).

Cool pavement is an effective technology to solve the UHI problem. The primary three types of cool pavements are (1) light color aggregates pavement, (2) permeable pavement, and (3) solar-reflective coating pavement. The [albedo](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/albedo "Learn more about albedo from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of normal [asphalt pavement](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/asphalt-pavements "Learn more about asphalt pavement from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is only about 5%. By using white or light color aggregates, the albedo can be increased to about 30% (Doulos et al., 2004). Recent case study results from Portland State University (Oregon, USA) show that if you increase the albedo of the pavement in a bare courtyard from 37% (black) to 91% (white), the [mean radiant temperature](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/mean-radiant-temperature "Learn more about mean radiant temperature from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") will increase 2.9°C, but the air temperature will decrease 1.3°C (Taleghani, 2014).

Cotana et al. (2014) believes that using high solar reflective surfaces is an effective way to mitigate the UHI problem and reduce [greenhouse gas emission](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/greenhouse-gas-emission "Learn more about greenhouse gas emission from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in urban areas. It has been found that albedo coatings can reduce CO 2 emissions. Simulation results indicated that a 115,000 m 2 high-albedo coating area could reduce 16,000 tons of CO 2eq, releasing and effectively mitigating [global warming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/global-warming "Learn more about global warming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") if applied for 30 years. Akbari and Matthews (2012) also estimated that using cool roofs and cool pavements could increase urban albedo by about 10%, and reduce CO 2 emissions by at least 40–160 Gt. Yu’s study claimed a similar result based on a [life cycle assessment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/life-cycle-assessment "Learn more about life cycle assessment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") model (Yu and Lu, 2012). Experimental results indicated that 4500 m 2 cool pavements in an urban parking lot could decrease the ambient temperature about 2°C, and at the same time decrease the surface temperature by 12°C during a typical summer day (Santamouris et al., 2012).

Rosenfeld et al. (1998) claimed that cool surface techniques could save at least half a billion dollars if used in the Log Angeles basin, and up to five billion dollars if applied in the entire U.S. by the year 2015. Akbari et al. (2009) estimated that urban albedo could be increased about 10% by using cool roofs and cool pavements, and the increased urban albedo could reduce 44 Gt of CO 2 emissions and save 1.1 trillion dollars worldwide.

To implement the high-reflectance [surface coating](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/surface-coating "Learn more about surface coating from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technology, proper _cool_ material selection plays the most important role. In general, the selection rule is governed by two main parameters: [solar reflectance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/solar-reflectance "Learn more about solar reflectance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and heat [emittance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/emittance "Learn more about emittance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Asaeda et al., 1996; Synnefa et al., 2006). Carnielo and Zinzi (2013) tested the emissivity and solar reflectance of five different albedo samples with colors of green, red, blue, gray, and off-white. It was found that, compared with [thermal emittance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/thermal-emittance "Learn more about thermal emittance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the effect of solar reflectance is more evident on mitigating the urban heat island phenomenon. The spectral emissivity values of those samples are similar to the normal [asphalt](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/asphalt "Learn more about asphalt from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which are all above 90%, while the solar reflectance of each sample varies a lot, with a maximum difference of about 61% compared to the normal asphalt sample.

The visible light reflectance of the coating is mainly governed by its visible color and [surface roughness](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/surface-roughness "Learn more about surface roughness from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which are determined by the [inorganic fillers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/inorganic-filler "Learn more about inorganic fillers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Uemoto et al. (2010) claimed that the visible light reflectance of the coating will decrease when the color is more dark than light. Other than the visible light reflectance, the near-infrared (NIR) light reflectance is also important for cool pavements. In the NIR reflective region, the colors of the materials are not the dominant factor. High NIR reflectance can be realized in various colors of inorganic fillers. Synnefa et al. (2011) investigated five asphalt [pavement overlay](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/pavement-overlays "Learn more about pavement overlay from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") coatings with various colors. The results indicated that the reflectance of the coatings with all colors are higher than the asphalt pavement without coatings. The NIR reflectance of these coating ranges from 27% (red and green) to 55%, which could maximally decrease the pavement temperature about 12°C compared with the noncoated asphalt pavement surface. Kinouchi et al. (2003) developed a new pavement coating with high reflectance and low brightness, which has a relatively low reflectance (20%) in the visible light region (350–750 nm) and high reflectance (83%) in the [NIR region](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/infrared-region "Learn more about NIR region from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (750–2100 nm). The results showed that this coating could decrease the pavement temperature by 15°C compared with normal asphalt pavement. Wan et al. (2012) also developed a cool pavement coating with high NIR reflectance (81%), low [thermal conductivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/thermal-conductivity "Learn more about thermal conductivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (0.252 W/m K), and high emissivity (82.8%). The results demonstrated that t

FULL CONTENT:
What Is an Urban Heat Island? | NASA Climate Kids

===============

[![Image 1: NASA Logo](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/resources/site/nasa_logo.png)](https://www.nasa.gov/)[![Image 2: NASA Climate Kids header image](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/resources/site/header_image.png)](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/)

[![Image 3: Illustration of a question mark that links to the Climate Kids Big Questions menu.](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/resources/site/menu_bigquestions.png) Big Questions](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/menu/big-questions/)[![Image 4: Illustration of a snowflake that links to the Climate Kids Weather & Climate menu.](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/resources/site/menu_weatherclimate.png) Weather & Climate](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/menu/weather-and-climate/)[![Image 5: Illustration of a cloud that links to the Climate Kids Atmosphere menu.](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/resources/site/menu_atmosphere.png) Atmosphere](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/menu/atmosphere/)[![Image 6: Illustration of water waves that links to the Climate Kids Water menu.](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/resources/site/menu_water.png) Water](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/menu/water/)[![Image 7: Illustration of windmills that links to the Climate Kids Energy menu.](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/resources/site/menu_energy.png) Energy](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/menu/energy/)[![Image 8: Illustration of a leaf that links to the Climate Kids Plants & Animals menu.](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/resources/site/menu_plantsanimals.png) Plants & Animals](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/menu/plants-and-animals/)

What Is an Urban Heat Island?
=============================

**The Short Answer:**

 An urban heat island occurs when a city experiences much warmer temperatures than nearby rural areas. The difference in temperature between urban and less-developed rural areas has to do with how well the surfaces in each environment absorb and hold heat. 

An **urban heat island** occurs when a city experiences much warmer temperatures than nearby rural areas.

![Image 9: An illustration of a bubble surrounding a city that represents an urban heat island.](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/heat-islands/heat-islands1.jpg)
An illustration of an urban heat island. Image credit: NASA/JPL-Caltech

Why does this happen?
---------------------

An **urban** area is a city. A **rural** area is out in the country. The sun’s heat and light reach the city and the country in the same way. The difference in temperature between urban and less-developed rural areas has to do with how well the surfaces in each environment absorb and hold heat.

If you travel to a rural area, you’ll probably find that most of the region is covered with plants. Grass, trees and farmland covered with crops, as far as the eye can see.

Plants take up water from the ground through their roots. Then, they store the water in their stems and leaves. The water eventually travels to small holes on the underside of leaves. There, the liquid water turns into water vapor and is released into the air. This process is called **transpiration**. It acts as nature’s air conditioner.

![Image 10: An illustration of the process of transpiration.](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/heat-islands/heat-islands2.jpg)
An illustration of the process of transpiration. Image credit: NASA/JPL-Caltech

### See it for yourself!

You can feel cooling transpiration at work on a hot summer day. On a sunny day, go outside and find a sidewalk that is right next to a patch of grass. Feel both surfaces. The grass should feel cooler on your skin than the pavement—and that’s mostly due to transpiration!

When you visit a big city, you won’t see many plants. Instead, you’ll see sidewalks, streets, parking lots and tall buildings. These structures are usually made up of materials such as cement, asphalt, brick, glass, steel and dark roofs.

What do urban building materials have in common?
------------------------------------------------

First of all, materials such as asphalt, steel, and brick are often very dark colors—like black, brown and grey. A dark object**absorbs**all wavelengths of light energy and converts them into heat, so the object gets warm. In contrast, a white object **reflects** all wavelengths of light. The light is not converted into heat and the temperature of the white object does not increase noticeably. Thus, dark objects—such as building materials—absorb heat from the sun.

![Image 11: An illustration of the process of transpiration.](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/heat-islands/heat-islands3.jpg)
Dark surfaces--whether a black t-shirt or an asphalt street--absorb the sun's heat, while lighter colored surfaces reflect heat from the sun. Credit: NASA/JPL-Caltech

To cool down urban heat islands, some cities are ‘lightening’ streets. This is done by covering black asphalt streets, parking lots, and dark roofs with a more reflective gray coating. These changes can drop urban air temperatures dramatically, especially during the heat of summer.

Planting gardens on urban rooftops can also help to cool down the city, too! In fact, a study in Los Angeles, California, calculated that changes like these would be enough to save close to $100 million per year in energy costs!

Urban building materials are another reason that urban areas trap heat. Many modern building materials are **impervious** surfaces. This means that water can’t flow through surfaces like a brick or a patch of cement like it would through a plant. Without a cycle of flowing and evaporating water, these surfaces have nothing to cool them down.

![Image 12: A photograph of skyscrapers in Chicago](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/heat-islands/chicago.jpg)
Skyscrapers in Chicago. Image credit: Flickr user GiuseppeYahoo Cortese

To help cool the heat island, builders can use materials that will allow water to flow through. These building materials—called permeable materials—promote the capture and flow of water, which cools urban regions.

What does it mean?
------------------

Urban heat islands are one of the easiest ways to see how human impact can change our planet. After all, sidewalks, parking lots and skyscrapers wouldn’t exist if humans weren’t there to build them. And although these structures are essential to city living, the heat islands they create can be dangerous for humans.

In the summer, New York City is about 7°F (4°C) hotter than its surrounding areas. That doesn’t seem like much, but these higher temperatures can cause people to become dehydrated or suffer from heat exhaustion. The hot temps also require more energy to operate fans and air conditioners. This can lead to power outages and a serious danger to public health.

But, there are things we can do to help cool the cities down. And NASA satellites can help to figure out where these cities are the hottest.

[![Image 13: Two satellite map images of New York City showing the cooling effects of plants on the city's heat](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/heat-islands/heat-islands4.png)](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/heat-islands/heat-islands4.png)
Caption: These images from the NASA/USGS satellite Landsat show the cooling effects of plants on New York City’s heat. On the left, areas of the map that are dark green have dense vegetation. Notice how these regions match up with the dark purple regions—those with the coolest temperatures—on the right. Image credit: Maps by Robert Simmon, using data from the Landsat Program.

Earth-observing satellites, such as Landsat and Suomi-NPP, can keep a close eye on the Earth’s vegetation and surface temperature. Scientists can use this information to track hotspots in cities across the planet. NASA scientists, with their global satellite views, are working to understand urban heat islands and help urban planners to build more energy efficient, cooler and safer cities.

**Big Questions**

[What does global climate change mean?](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/climate-change-meaning/)[What is the big deal with carbon?](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/carbon/)[What is the greenhouse effect?](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/greenhouse-effect/)[How do we know the climate is changing?](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/climate-change-evidence/)[What is happening to the oceans?](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/ocean/)[What else do we need to find out?](https://climatekids.nasa.gov/nasa-research/)

**Weather & Climate**

--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://image.slidesharecdn.com/urbanheatisland-140609113151-phpapp02/95/urban-heat-island-4-638.jpg?cb=1402313592
DESCRIPTION: The illustration depicts how urban areas experience higher temperatures, reaching up to 92°F (32°C), due to heat absorption by buildings and surfaces, while surrounding green spaces and green walls help dissipate heat and cool the environment, exemplifying the Urban Heat Island effect.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://skepticalscience.com/graphics/UrbanHeatIsland_med.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates how urban areas absorb more heat due to surface properties, leading to less cooling from evaporation and plant transpiration, resulting in greater ground storage of rainfall compared to rural areas.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://d3f9k0n15ckvhe.cloudfront.net/wp-content/uploads/2020/09/UrbanHeatIslandIllustration-FINAL-1024x826-1.jpg
DESCRIPTION: A vibrant illustration depicts an urban environment characterized by heat-absorbing surfaces like buildings and asphalt, contrasted with cooler suburban green spaces that feature trees, highlighting the concept of an Urban Heat Island and its effects on surrounding temperatures.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://www.insightsonindia.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/urban.jpg
DESCRIPTION: an illustration of urban heat island effect

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://pwonlyias.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Picture24.webp
DESCRIPTION: The description illustrates the urban heat island effect, highlighting how urban materials like concrete and asphalt retain heat, leading to increased temperatures and adverse health consequences for vulnerable populations, while green areas help keep cities cooler.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Urban Heat Islands (UHIs) are urban areas significantly warmer than their rural surroundings due to human activities, dense construction, and materials that absorb and retain heat.
Understanding UHIs is crucial for urban planning as it affects energy consumption, air quality, and climate resilience, prompting the need for sustainable design and mitigation strategies.

enriched_points.point_title: Impact on public health and environment
enriched_points.expanded_content: UHIs negatively impact public health, increasing heat-related illnesses, while also exacerbating air pollution and reducing water quality, which threatens urban biodiversity and overall environmental health.
